content.text: is the core computational primitive of every transformer model. It lets each token dynamically decide how much to rely on every other token in the sequence to form its representation — replacing the fixed receptive fields of convolutions and the serial dependencies of recurrent networks with a single, parallel, content-driven lookup.
Attention reformulates sequence processing as a differentiable database query. Given a sequence of
input vectors, the mechanism derives three matrices from learned linear projections of the input: a
. Each query vector asks "what do I need?"; each key vector advertises "what do I contain?"; and each value vector supplies the actual content to be mixed.
The output for each query is a weighted average of all values, where the weights are determined by the compatibility of that query with each key:
scaling factor prevents the dot products from growing large in magnitude when

Rather than running a single attention function, transformers run
parallel attention heads, each with its own learned projections
. Each head can specialize in a different type of relationship. The outputs of all heads are concatenated and projected back to model dimension via

Every capability that makes LLMs useful in agentic systems — following multi-step instructions, referencing earlier context, resolving ambiguous pronouns, linking a variable definition to its use — is a direct product of attention. Without it, a model processing the prompt "Use the API key from the previous message" would have no mechanism to reach back and retrieve what "the previous message" contains.

For agent builders, understanding attention explains concrete latency and quality phenomena: why performance can degrade on very long context windows (quadratic scaling), why placing critical instructions near the top or bottom of the system prompt can differ from placing them in the middle ("lost in the middle" effect), and why multi-head attention enables parallel extraction of syntactic and semantic relationships simultaneously.

across sequence positions during training, enabling fast gradient computation on modern GPUs

— they can be inspected to understand what the model attends to (with caveats)

Attention weights are often misread as explanations: high weight on a token does not necessarily mean it is the causal reason for the output

Decoder-only LLMs (GPT family, Llama, Claude's underlying architecture) enforce that position
to the upper triangle of the score matrix before softmax, so those entries become 0 after softmax. The result is a strictly lower-triangular attention weight matrix:

Causal mask for n=4 (✓ = attend, ✗ = blocked):
      j=1  j=2  j=3  j=4
i=1    ✓    ✗    ✗    ✗
i=2    ✓    ✓    ✗    ✗

The role of the scaling factor
is large, random query and key vectors have dot products with standard deviation
rather than 1. Large dot products push softmax towards a one-hot distribution (all weight on one token), producing near-zero gradients everywhere else. Dividing by
keeps the input to softmax in a well-conditioned range.

The diagram above shows the six-stage pipeline of scaled dot-product attention. Input embeddings are simultaneously projected into three representation spaces (Q, K, V). The query-key dot product produces a score matrix; an optional causal mask zeros out future positions before softmax normalizes each row into a probability distribution. The final output is computed as the weighted sum of value vectors. In multi-head attention, this pipeline runs
times in parallel; the concatenated outputs pass through a final linear projection
before the residual connection and layer normalization.
: document QA, code completion, multi-turn agent conversations
in encoder-decoder models for translation, summarization, and retrieval-augmented generation
Building custom attention variants: sliding-window attention for very long sequences, cross-document attention in multi-document RAG
Debugging agent prompts — understanding attention helps explain why a model ignores a constraint placed in the middle of a long system prompt
rolling your own attention from scratch in production; use
(PyTorch 2.0+) or Flash Attention 2/3, which fuse the operations in hardware-efficient CUDA kernels

The implementation below covers single-head scaled dot-product attention, then builds the standard multi-head wrapper with split/merge head operations. This is the reference implementation — in practice, prefer
or Flash Attention for production workloads.

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


def scaled_dot_product_attention(
    q: torch.Tensor,   # (batch, heads, seq, d_k)
    k: torch.Tensor,
    v: torch.Tensor,
    mask: torch.Tensor | None = None,  # (batch, 1, seq, seq) or (batch, heads, seq, seq)
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """Return (output, attention_weights)."""
    d_k = q.size(-1)
    # Step 1: raw scores — (batch, heads, seq_q, seq_k)
    scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    # Step 2: apply causal / padding mask
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf"))
    # Step 3: softmax over keys dimension
    attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    # Step 4: weighted sum of values
    output = torch.matmul(attn_weights, v)
    return output, attn_weights


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Standard multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017)."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads

        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def _split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # (batch, seq, d_model) -> (batch, heads, seq, d_k)
        batch, seq, _ = x.size()
        return x.view(batch, seq, self.num_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def forward(
        self,
        query: torch.Tensor,
        key: torch.Tensor,
        value: torch.Tensor,
        mask: torch.Tensor | None = None,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        q = self._split_heads(self.W_q(query))
        k = self._split_heads(self.W_k(key))
        v = self._split_heads(self.W_v(value))

        out, _ = scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, mask)

        # Merge heads: (batch, heads, seq, d_k) -> (batch, seq, d_model)
        batch, _, seq, _ = out.size()
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch, seq, -1)
        return self.W_o(out)


# Quick smoke test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=512, num_heads=8)
    x = torch.randn(2, 16, 512)   # batch=2, seq_len=16
    out = mha(x, x, x)
    print(out.shape)               # torch.Size([2, 16, 512])
The following snippet shows the concrete difference between bidirectional and causal attention on a small toy sequence, making it easy to verify mask behavior:

import math, torch, torch.nn.functional as F


def build_causal_mask(seq_len: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Return a (1, 1, seq_len, seq_len) boolean mask where True = allowed."""
    return torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, dtype=torch.bool)).unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)


def visualise_attention(
    q: torch.Tensor,   # (seq, d_k)
    k: torch.Tensor,
    v: torch.Tensor,
    causal: bool = False,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """Single-head attention for visualisation; returns (output, weights)."""
    d_k = q.size(-1)
    raw = (q @ k.T) / math.sqrt(d_k)           # (seq_q, seq_k)
    if causal:
        n = raw.size(0)
        mask = build_causal_mask(n).squeeze()    # (n, n)
        raw = raw.masked_fill(~mask, float("-inf"))
    weights = F.softmax(raw, dim=-1)
    output = weights @ v
    return output, weights


# Example: 5 tokens, d_k = 8
torch.manual_seed(42)
n, dk = 5, 8
Q = torch.randn(n, dk)
K = torch.randn(n, dk)
V = torch.randn(n, dk)

_, w_full = visualise_attention(Q, K, V, causal=False)
_, w_causal = visualise_attention(Q, K, V, causal=True)

print("Full (bidirectional) attention weights:")
print(w_full.round(decimals=3))
print()
print("Causal (autoregressive) attention weights:")
print(w_causal.round(decimals=3))
# Lower-triangular: future tokens show 0 weight
memory by processing keys and values in tiles, maintaining a running softmax numerator and denominator without materializing the full
score matrix. The following pedagogical implementation illustrates the online-softmax tiling idea:

"""
Flash-Attention–style chunked attention in pure Python (pedagogical).
Real Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) fuses the softmax + matmul
in CUDA SRAM; this illustrates the tiling idea without CUDA.
"""
import math
import torch


def chunked_attention(
    Q: torch.Tensor,   # (seq, d_k)
    K: torch.Tensor,
    V: torch.Tensor,
    chunk_size: int = 64,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """O(n) memory attention via online softmax; O(n²) compute."""
    seq, d_k = Q.shape
    scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(d_k)
    output = torch.zeros_like(Q)
    row_max = torch.full((seq,), float("-inf"))
    row_sum = torch.zeros(seq)

    for start in range(0, seq, chunk_size):
        end = min(start + chunk_size, seq)
        k_chunk = K[start:end]         # (chunk, d_k)
        v_chunk = V[start:end]         # (chunk, d_k)

        scores = (Q @ k_chunk.T) * scale  # (seq, chunk)
        chunk_max = scores.max(dim=-1).values

        # Numerically stable online softmax update
        new_max = torch.maximum(row_max, chunk_max)
        exp_scores = torch.exp(scores - new_max.unsqueeze(1))  # (seq, chunk)
        exp_shift = torch.exp(row_max - new_max)

        output = output * exp_shift.unsqueeze(1) + exp_scores @ v_chunk
        row_sum = row_sum * exp_shift + exp_scores.sum(dim=-1)
        row_max = new_max

    return output / row_sum.unsqueeze(1)


torch.manual_seed(0)
n, d = 128, 64
Q = torch.randn(n, d)
K = torch.randn(n, d)
V = torch.randn(n, d)
out = chunked_attention(Q, K, V, chunk_size=32)
print(f"Output shape: {out.shape}")   # (128, 64)

Consider an agentic system prompting Claude or GPT-4 to book a flight. The system message contains airline policy rules, the user turn contains the destination and dates, and an earlier assistant turn contains a retrieved itinerary. When generating the JSON tool call, the attention mechanism:
Queries from the token being generated (e.g., the departure airport field) attend to keys from the user's stated destination — linking the two across potentially hundreds of tokens
Later heads attend to the policy rules in the system message to verify the booking is within allowed fare classes
Causal masking ensures the model cannot "look ahead" at tokens not yet generated, maintaining valid autoregressive generation
This is not metaphorical — the intermediate attention weights during generation can be extracted and logged to observe which context positions the model relies on for each token it produces.

In multi-GPU inference, partition heads across devices (tensor parallelism) rather than duplicating the full model on each device
Attention is the primary bottleneck for long-context inference. The standard implementation materializes an
memory. For a context of 128 k tokens with 96 heads of dimension 128 in float16, the attention score matrices alone require approximately 3 GB. Practical solutions include:
stores computed key and value tensors for all previous positions so that each new token only requires one additional row of scores to be computed. This reduces per-step attention cost from
, but the cache grows linearly with sequence length and can become the dominant memory consumer for long agent sessions.
— standard PyTorch implementation; supports batch-first, key/padding masks, custom attn_mask
— PyTorch 2.0+, dispatches to Flash Attention on CUDA, memory-efficient attention on CPU
(Dao et al.) — state-of-the-art fused CUDA kernel; 2-8x faster than standard, O(n) memory
— Meta's library of efficient attention variants including memory-efficient attention, local/sparse patterns
— hook-based toolkit for mechanistic interpretability; access attention weights, residual stream states, activation patches
— interactive visualizer for BERT and GPT attention heads across layers
— expressive tensor rearrangement library that makes head-splitting and head-merging operations readable

State-space models like Mamba achieve linear compute through selective state compression rather than explicit pairwise interactions. They can match attention on many benchmarks but sacrifice the ability to selectively retrieve from arbitrary positions in context — a key capability for agent systems that must reference specific earlier tool outputs or user constraints.
: adversarial text injected into tool results can attract disproportionate attention weight, causing the model to override earlier instructions — always sanitize and quote untrusted tool outputs
: empirical studies show LLM attention is stronger at the beginning and end of context; critical constraints placed in the middle of a 32 k token prompt may receive less weight and be ignored
: initial tokens (especially BOS) accumulate anomalously high attention mass in deep models, regardless of semantic relevance — do not rely on positional heuristics alone for grounding
: in shared inference infrastructure, a leaking KV cache would expose prior queries to future users — ensure caches are isolated per user session
and use float32 for softmax in mixed-precision training — float16 softmax on large
is a common source of NaN losses

Attention is a differentiable search engine embedded inside every transformer layer. Queries are search terms, keys are document indices, values are the documents themselves — and softmax is the ranking function that decides how much of each document to read and blend together.

Extend this further: in a 96-layer GPT model, this search-and-blend operation runs 96 times per forward pass, each layer refining a richer representation than the last. Early layers match syntactic patterns; middle layers capture semantic relationships; later layers encode task-relevant structure used for the final prediction.
: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QKᵀ / √dₖ)V — a scaled, soft database lookup
Three projections (Q, K, V) are learned weight matrices applied to the same or different input sequences

Causal masking converts bidirectional attention into autoregressive generation by blocking future positions

KV cache is the mechanism that makes multi-step agent inference practical — compute once, reuse keys and values for each new token
Agent builders should understand that critical instructions should appear early or at the end of context, not buried in the middle, due to attention weight distribution
